claims.claim_text: GitHub Actions supports reusable workflows and matrix strategies.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do
claims.rationale: GitHub's primary documentation directly covers reusable workflows and matrix use, but the docs host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: GitHub supports self-hosted runners on Kubernetes via Actions Runner Controller (ARC).
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do; Quiz Question 7 answer
claims.rationale: The official ARC repository explicitly identifies ARC as the Kubernetes controller for GitHub Actions self-hosted runners.

claims.claim_text: GitHub's security platform includes CodeQL, secret scanning, and dependency review.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do; section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: GitHub's official security plans page directly lists CodeQL, secret scanning, and the dependency review action.

claims.claim_text: GitHub Actions OIDC can be used for secure cloud access with short-lived credentials instead of long-lived secrets.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do
claims.rationale: The official GitHub Actions OIDC documentation directly supports this claim, but the docs host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A pre-acquisition audit of a 150-developer fintech found 847 AWS keys, 234 database strings, 23 PII files, and froze a $180M deal.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening anecdote paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The security consultant's report landed like a bomb in the CTO's inbox. A routine pre-acquisition audit of a 150-developer fintech startup had uncovered 847 AWS access keys, 234 database connection strings, and 23 files containing customer PII—all committed to Git history over four years. The acquiring company's legal team froze the $180M deal pending remediation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A pre-acquisition security review can uncover years of leaked credentials and sensitive data in Git history, forcing remediation work before a deal can close.
claims.rationale: This is a highly specific unattributed war story with precise counts and deal value, so the false specifics need to be removed.

claims.claim_text: Within two weeks GHAS enabled remediation, blocked three new secret commits, found SQL injection issues, and still cost $340K plus a $15M price cut.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening anecdote paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: Within two weeks, the same repository fleet had GitHub Advanced Security enabled. Secret scanning flagged the historical exposure. Push protection blocked three more credential commits in the first week. CodeQL found SQL injection vulnerabilities in their payment processing code. The deal closed—but only after $340K in emergency remediation and a $15M reduction in acquisition price.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Security tooling such as secret scanning, push protection, and code scanning can help teams uncover historical exposure and stop new risky commits, but late adoption still leads to expensive remediation.
claims.rationale: This is another unattributed incident narrative with fake-precise remediation numbers and timeline details.

claims.claim_text: Using GitHub properly rather than superficially can prevent incidents worth millions of dollars.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, closing sentence before Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: The difference between "using GitHub" and "using GitHub properly" is millions of dollars in prevented incidents.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The difference between basic repository hosting and disciplined use of GitHub's security features can materially change an organization's risk.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced financial-impact claim with no defensible underlying measurement.

claims.claim_text: GitHub bought Semmle in 2019 for an estimated $400-500M after 13 years of development and became the world's largest free SAST provider overnight.
claims.anchor_text: - **CodeQL came from a $500M acquisition** — In 2019, GitHub bought Semmle, the company behind CodeQL, for an undisclosed sum (estimated $400-500M). Semmle had spent 13 years developing semantic code analysis, originally for finding bugs in safety-critical systems. GitHub made it free for public repos overnight, instantly becoming the world's largest free SAST provider.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **CodeQL came from Semmle** — GitHub acquired Semmle in 2019 and incorporated CodeQL into its security platform, later making CodeQL code scanning available for public repositories.
claims.rationale: The acquisition happened, but the estimated price, timeline, and superlative are not cleanly supported here and should be removed.

claims.claim_text: In 2023 GitHub secret scanning and AWS notification prevented a $14M crypto exchange hot-wallet incident within seconds.
claims.anchor_text: - **Secret scanning prevented a cryptocurrency exchange hack** — In 2023, a developer at a major crypto exchange accidentally committed an AWS key with access to hot wallets containing $14M in assets. GitHub's partner program notified AWS within 30 seconds. AWS auto-revoked the key 47 seconds before automated scanners on the dark web detected the commit. The exchange never knew how close they came.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Secret scanning can reduce secret-exposure risk** — GitHub's partner alerts and push protection can help teams and providers react quickly when credentials are exposed.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed security incident with exact seconds, asset values, and claimed outcomes that are not supportable from a primary source here.

claims.claim_text: GitHub says 46% of code is AI-generated at adopting companies, a 2024 Stanford study found Copilot users 40% more likely to introduce vulnerabilities, and GitHub cut vulnerable suggestions by 65%.
claims.anchor_text: - **Copilot's "46% of code" stat has a dark side** — While GitHub reports that 46% of code at adopting companies is AI-generated, a 2024 Stanford study found that developers using Copilot were 40% more likely to introduce security vulnerabilities. GitHub responded by adding security-focused prompts, reducing vulnerable suggestions by 65%—but the lesson stands: AI-generated code needs AI-powered review.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **AI-generated code still needs review** — Copilot can accelerate development, but teams still need code review and security checks because generated code can introduce defects or vulnerabilities.
claims.rationale: This is a bundle of specific statistics and causal claims that should not remain without exact primary support for each number.

claims.claim_text: GitHub Actions caused CircleCI's decline, from a $1.7B valuation and 30M builds/month in 2019 to layoffs after Actions reached 10x that volume by 2023.
claims.anchor_text: - **GitHub Actions killed CircleCI's growth** — When GitHub Actions launched in 2019, CircleCI was valued at $1.7B and processing 30M builds/month. By 2023, Actions processed 10x that volume. CircleCI laid off 25% of staff in January 2023. The lesson: vertical integration wins.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Bundled CI/CD changed the market** — GitHub Actions increased competitive pressure on standalone CI vendors by integrating automation directly into the hosting platform.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed business-history narrative with precise market and staffing claims that should be collapsed to the general lesson.

claims.claim_text: CodeQL works by querying a semantic representation of code rather than relying only on simple pattern matching.
claims.rationale: The authoritative CodeQL documentation supports the claim directly, but the host is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Push protection scans pushes for detected secrets and blocks them before they are committed to repository history.
claims.span_hint: section: Secret Scanning with Push Protection; Quiz Question 3 answer
claims.rationale: GitHub's official security plans page explicitly states that push protection blocks secrets before they reach code.

claims.claim_text: Reusable workflows are complete workflows/jobs, while composite actions bundle reusable steps within a job.
claims.rationale: GitHub's workflow duplication docs directly compare reusable workflows and composite actions, but the docs host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Enterprise Managed Users lets an enterprise provision and control users through its IdP and SCIM, and restricts those managed accounts to enterprise-controlled access.
claims.span_hint: section: Enterprise Features; Quiz Question 6 answer
claims.rationale: GitHub's EMU documentation directly covers lifecycle management, authentication, and access restrictions, but the docs host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Managed user usernames are formatted as an IdP-derived name plus an enterprise shortcode suffix, not `@shortcode_username`.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Have usernames derived from the IdP username with an enterprise shortcode suffix, such as `@mona-cat_octo`
claims.rationale: The current GitHub EMU username format is suffix-based, so the example format in the module is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: The GHAS quiz answer says GHAS is free for public repositories and requires Enterprise Cloud for private repos.
claims.anchor_text: It's free for public repositories and requires Enterprise Cloud for private repos.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Several GitHub code security features are available for public repositories, while private-repository security features depend on your plan and purchased security add-ons.
claims.rationale: The current packaging is feature-specific and this sentence overstates GHAS as a single public-vs-private price boundary.

claims.claim_text: The module presents an 'Individual ($10/mo)' Copilot plan with only IDE completion and chat.
claims.anchor_text: - **Individual ($10/mo)**: Code completion and chat in IDE
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Individual plans**: GitHub currently offers Free, Pro, and Pro+ plans for individual developers, with features that vary by plan.
claims.rationale: GitHub's individual Copilot lineup has changed, so this older single-plan framing is stale.

claims.claim_text: The module's Copilot Business bullet combines current pricing with a broad feature list including an unsupported 'no training on your code' claim.
claims.anchor_text: - **Business ($19/mo)**: + Policy management, file exclusions, audit logs, no training on your code
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Business ($19/mo)**: Business adds centralized administration features such as policy controls, content exclusion, and audit visibility for organizations.
claims.rationale: The pricing is current, but the bundled feature wording includes a strong policy claim that needs more precise sourcing than the module provides.

claims.claim_text: The module's Copilot Enterprise bullet says Enterprise includes GitHub chat, PR summaries, knowledge bases, and fine-tuning.
claims.anchor_text: - **Enterprise ($39/mo)**: + Chat on github.com, PR summaries, knowledge bases, fine-tuning
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Enterprise ($39/mo)**: Enterprise adds organization-wide Copilot capabilities on GitHub.com, while specific enterprise-only features continue to evolve over time.
claims.rationale: This line is stale because knowledge bases were retired in 2025 and the enterprise feature bundle has shifted.

claims.claim_text: The module says many secret-scanning partners automatically revoke leaked credentials and that the partner program includes named providers plus '100+ others.'
claims.anchor_text: When GitHub detects a secret (like an AWS key or Stripe API key), it notifies the service provider. Many partners automatically revoke the credential before it can be exploited. This "secret scanning partner program" includes AWS, Azure, GCP, Stripe, Twilio, and 100+ others.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When GitHub detects a supported secret pattern, it can notify participating providers, which may then decide whether to revoke, rotate, or otherwise secure the exposed credential.
claims.rationale: GitHub's docs support partner notification, but not this blanket auto-revocation phrasing and unsupported provider-count summary.

claims.claim_text: The key takeaway says GHAS is free for public repositories.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **GHAS is free for public repos** - Enable it immediately on open source projects
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **GitHub provides important code security features for public repos** - Enable the relevant security features on open source projects as early as possible.
claims.rationale: The current product packaging is more nuanced than this blanket statement, so the takeaway should avoid overstating the pricing model.

claims.claim_text: Rulesets are more flexible than classic branch protection because multiple rulesets can apply at once.
claims.span_hint: section: Enterprise Features, Repository Rulesets; section: Key Takeaways, item 8
claims.rationale: GitHub's rulesets documentation explicitly describes multiple simultaneously-applicable rulesets and their advantages, but the docs host is off-allowlist.

further_reading.title: GitHub Security Plans
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical current overview of GitHub's Code Security and Secret Protection features and packaging.

further_reading.title: Actions Runner Controller
further_reading.why_relevant: Official ARC repository for running and scaling GitHub Actions self-hosted runners on Kubernetes.

further_reading.title: GitHub Copilot Plans & Pricing
further_reading.why_relevant: Current Copilot plan lineup and pricing, useful because several module Copilot references are stale.

further_reading.title: dependency-review-action
further_reading.why_relevant: Official action repository describing dependency review behavior, availability, and configuration.

notes: Excluded code blocks, ASCII diagrams, and markdown tables per schema. Several GitHub documentation pages that directly support accurate claims are on docs.github.com or codeql.github.com, so they were marked as needs_allowlist_expansion. Copilot knowledge bases are stale in this module as of April 20, 2026 because GitHub retired them on November 1, 2025.
